name: rad-planner
description:
**Plan a project before you write any code — or replan after a pivot.** rad-planner walks you through a structured 6-phase workflow — strategic discovery, stack evaluation, task breakdown with explicit dependency graph, risk review, human approval, export — and captures the result as a set of project documents a fresh AI session can pick up and execute without conversation history.

`/plan` does real work:
- Asks strategic discovery questions ("biggest technical risk?", "non-obvious truths about this project's reality?", "what existing systems must this integrate with?")
- Evaluates your tech stack against the Golden Path proficiency matrix; verifies current framework versions via Context7 / web search
- Breaks work into milestones with explicit task dependencies (a real DAG, not a flat list)
- Scores complexity 1–10 per task; auto-expands anything > 7 into subtasks
- Defines a runnable validation command + rollback procedure for every task
- Runs the risk-assessor agent (anti-patterns, TDD strategy, architectural concerns) — up to 3 iterations, with explicit APPROVE / REVISE / RETHINK verdicts
- Inserts checkpoints after every milestone

The approved plan persists to your project root as 7 files:
- **PRD.md** — what we're building and why
- **ARCHITECTURE.md** — how it fits together (with component diagram)
- **ASSUMPTIONS.md** — non-obvious truths that won't survive context compaction ("no users yet", "single-tenant only", "sensitive data — no real values in repo")
- **DECISIONS.md** — sequence-numbered, append-only architecture decisions with supersession history
- **PLAN.md** — milestones, tasks, checkpoints, target files
- **tasks.md** — machine-readable task graph
- **CLAUDE-FRAGMENT.md** — strategic `@-imports` that rad-session's `/init` merges into CLAUDE.md

Modes: `/plan` (full workflow), `--lite` (small single-milestone work, skips stack and risk passes), `--reboot` (audit current code, archive prior docs, regenerate anchored to reality, mark superseded decisions), `--validate` (cheap gap-check on the doc set, no agents dispatched).

Python validators back the workflow with mechanical checks templates alone can't catch: `plan-lint.py` flags DAG cycles, phantom dependencies, missing fields, and vague validation language ("verify it works", "tbd"). `validate-json.py` enforces the JSON contract for subagent outputs. Three Opus-default agents handle stack evaluation (`stack-advisor`), planning orchestration (`plan-architect`), and risk review (`risk-assessor`).

Pairs naturally with rad-session for the full project lifecycle, but works standalone.
